content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Three months ago I thought blockchain voting was basically solved. Put votes on a chain, make it immutable, done. No more rigged elections, no more “lost” ballots, no more trusting a black box.

I was wrong, and the way I was wrong is the part nobody warns you about.
I was building a voting system for a university research project. Smart contract on the backend, simple web interface on the front, votes recorded on-chain so nothing could be quietly altered after the fact. On paper it looked clean. In practice it took me about three weeks to realize I had built something that was secure and completely useless, because anyone could see exactly who voted for what.
That sentence took me a while to actually sit with. I had solved the wrong problem.
The part everyone gets right
Smart contracts are genuinely good at the thing they’re supposed to do here. Once a vote is written to the chain, it cannot be quietly edited, deleted, or rewritten by an administrator who doesn’t like the result. That part of the blockchain pitch is true and it matters. Traditional electronic voting systems have a single point of failure: whoever controls the database controls the outcome, or at least controls the appearance of the outcome. A…
